text.title: Required Claims and Lifetime of an Approval Token
text.summary: An approval token is a short-lived, scope-bound, single-use signed JWT — not a Slack reaction.

section.title: Closed claim set, short expiry
section.p: The token must encode exactly enough information for the agent's tool layer to verify the human authorized this specific call, on this specific target, within this specific incident, and not at any other time.

rule.statement: An approval token MUST be a JWT (RS256 / EdDSA) with at minimum these claims: `iss` (approval service), `sub` (human approver identity from SSO/IdP), `aud` (agent identity), `iat`, `exp` (≤300 seconds from `iat`), `jti` (unique, recorded for replay protection), `scope` (object: `tool`, `target`, `args_hash`), `correlation_id` (incident or task ID). The agent's tool layer MUST verify signature, `aud` match, `exp` not passed, `jti` not seen before, `scope.tool` matches the call, `scope.target` matches the call, and `scope.args_hash` matches `sha256(canonical(args))`. Failing any check, the call MUST be refused and an audit record emitted.
rule.rationale: Short expiry plus single-use plus args-hash binding means a token leaked or socially harvested cannot be replayed against a different call — the cryptographic envelope is the audit evidence.

example.note: 5-minute window, scoped to the exact target, replay-blocked by jti, bound to the incident.
Not signed, not scoped, replayable forever, edits invisible — no audit value.

antipattern.description: Issuing a long-lived (24h+) token with `scope: "*"` to "make the on-call workflow easier".
antipattern.reason: The first leaked or socially harvested token grants the agent unlimited writes for a day; the entire identity boundary collapses to one bearer string.

section.reference: Decoded payload showing the required claims.
